content.problemStatement:
Chef has a **strictly increasing** sequence AAA of length NNN that he wants to convert into an arithmetic progression†^{\dagger}†.

In one operation, Chef can choose some integer, and then insert it anywhere into the array.

Find the minimum operations required for Chef to achieve his goal. It can be proven that it is possible under the constraints of this problem.

†^{\dagger}† A sequence AAA of length NNN is called an Arithmetic progression if Ai−Ai−1A_i - A_{i - 1}Ai​−Ai−1​ is constant for all 1≤i<N1 \le i < N1≤i<N. For example, [1,3,5,7,9][1, 3, 5, 7, 9][1,3,5,7,9] and [3][3][3] are arithmetic progressions but not [1,5,7,9][1, 5, 7, 9][1,5,7,9].


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN - the size of the array.
The second line contains NNN integers - A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the minimum operations needed for Chef.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1 : The given sequence is already an Arithmetic progression.
Test Case 2 : Chef adds the integers 333 and 555 between 111 and 777 to get the sequence [1,3,5,7,9][1, 3, 5, 7, 9][1,3,5,7,9] which is an arithmetic progression.
